preamble.summary: A miktam of David expressing radical satisfaction in God as the psalmist's sole inheritance and portion. The psalm moves from a plea for protection through a confession of exclusive devotion to the LORD, past a rejection of other gods, into the language of deep contentment — the boundary lines have fallen in pleasant places. It culminates in one of the most extraordinary statements in the Hebrew Bible: 'You will not abandon my soul to Sheol, nor let your faithful one see the pit.' The psalm ends with the vision of fullness of joy in God's presence and pleasures at his right hand forever.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 16 is unique in the Psalter for its sustained tone of quiet, untroubled confidence. There is no lament, no enemy, no crisis to resolve. The psalm is pure satisfaction — the contentment of a person who has found that God is enough. The language of inheritance runs through the entire poem: the LORD is 'my portion' (chelqi) and 'my cup' (kosi), and the 'boundary lines' (chavalim) have fallen in pleasant places. This is land-allotment language: when Israel divided Canaan, each tribe received its nachalah ('inheritance, allotted portion'). The Levites received no land — their inheritance was the LORD himself (Numbers 18:20, Deuteronomy 10:9). The psalmist adopts Levitical theology and makes it personal: my allotted portion is not a field or a vineyard but God himself. Verse 10 — 'you will not abandon my soul to Sheol' — becomes one of the most theologically generative verses in the Hebrew Bible. Peter quotes it in Acts 2:25-28 as a prophecy of Christ's resurrection, arguing that David spoke of someone whose body would not see corruption.
preamble.friction: Verses 3-4 are among the most textually difficult in the Psalter. The Hebrew is compressed and possibly corrupt. The general sense — that the psalmist delights in the 'holy ones' (qedoshim) who are in the land while rejecting those who chase after other gods — is clear, but the grammar resists smooth translation. The word atstabotam (v. 4) may mean 'their sorrows' or 'their idols' (from etsev, 'pain/idol'). The phrase 'I will not pour out their drink offerings of blood' suggests rejection of pagan rituals involving blood libations. Verse 10's claim about not being abandoned to Sheol pushes against the dominant Old Testament understanding that all the dead go to Sheol regardless of righteousness. The psalm seems to reach toward something beyond the standard view — a hope that the relationship with God is strong enough to survive death itself.
preamble.connections: The inheritance language connects to Numbers 18:20 (the Levites' portion) and Psalm 73:26 ('God is the portion of my heart forever'). Peter's quotation of vv. 8-11 in Acts 2:25-31 makes this psalm central to early Christian resurrection theology. Paul cites verse 10 in Acts 13:35 with the same interpretive move. The 'path of life' (orach chayyim, v. 11) reappears in Proverbs 5:6 and 15:24. The 'pleasures at your right hand forever' (v. 11) echoes Psalm 110:1, where the king sits at God's right hand.

verses.text_hebrew: מִכְתָּ֥ם לְדָוִ֑ד שׇׁמְרֵ֥נִי אֵ֝֗ל כִּי־חָסִ֥יתִי בָֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Preserve me, O God: for in thee do I put my trust.
verses.rendering:
A miktam of David.
Protect me, God, for I have taken refuge in you.
translator_notes: miktam appears six times in the Psalter (Psalms 16, 56-60) and nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible. The Septuagint translates it as stelographia ('inscription on a pillar'). The meaning remains uncertain, but the word may relate to ketem ('gold') or to katam ('to cover, conceal').

key_terms.semantic_range: inscription, golden poem, atonement song — exact meaning uncertain
key_terms.note: The term is left untranslated because no proposed meaning commands scholarly consensus. It functions as a genre marker for a specific type of psalm associated with David, appearing in Psalms 16, 56, 57, 58, 59, and 60.

verses.text_hebrew: אָמַ֣רְתְּ לַ֭יהוָה אֲדֹנָ֣י אָ֑תָּה טוֹבָתִ֗י בַּל־עָלֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: O my soul, thou hast said unto the LORD, Thou art my Lord: my goodness extendeth not to thee;
verses.rendering:
I said to the LORD, 'You are my Lord;
I have no good apart from you.'
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist addresses the LORD with a double title: YHVH (the covenant name) and Adonai ('my Lord, my master'). The confession that follows — tovati bal alekha — is notoriously difficult. It may mean 'my good is not apart from you' (all my good comes from you) or 'my good does not extend beyond you' (you are my complete good) or even 'I have nothing good that is not yours.' All readings converge on a single theological claim: the LORD is the totality of the psalmist's good. There is no goodness in the psalmist's life that exists independently of God. This is not a statement of self-deprecation but of radical God-centeredness: every good thing traces back to you.
translator_notes: The Hebrew of this verse is among the most debated in the Psalter. The second-person feminine form amarte is unusual (either an archaic form or addressed to the psalmist's own soul). The phrase tovati bal alekha has generated at least four major interpretive traditions. Our rendering follows the dominant reading: 'I have no good apart from you.'

verses.text_kjv: But to the saints that are in the earth, and to the excellent, in whom is all my delight.
verses.rendering:
As for the holy ones in the land,
they are the noble ones in whom is all my delight.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist turns from God to God's people. The qedoshim ('holy ones, set-apart ones') in the land are those who, like the psalmist, have committed themselves exclusively to the LORD. They are called addirei ('noble ones, mighty ones, majestic ones') — a term of honor. The psalmist's chefets ('delight, pleasure, desire') is entirely in them. This creates a social vision: the person who finds all their good in God (v. 2) finds all their delight in God's people (v. 3). Devotion to God produces devotion to the community of the faithful. The theology is relational at every level — upward toward God and outward toward the saints.
translator_notes: The identity of the qedoshim ('holy ones') is debated. They may be human saints (faithful Israelites), heavenly beings (as in some readings of Deuteronomy 33:2-3), or both. The context — 'in the land' (ba-arets) — favors human referents: the faithful community in Israel.

verses.text_hebrew: יִרְבּ֥וּ עַצְּבוֹתָם֮ אַחֵ֢ר מָ֫הָ֥רוּ בַּל־אַסִּ֣יךְ נִסְכֵּיהֶ֣ם מִדָּ֑ם וּֽבַל־אֶשָּׂ֥א אֶת־שְׁ֝מוֹתָ֗ם עַל־שְׂפָתָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Their sorrows shall be multiplied that hasten after another god: their drink offerings of blood will I not offer, nor take up their names into my lips.
verses.rendering:
The sorrows of those who chase after another god will multiply.
I will not pour out their drink offerings of blood,
nor will I take their names on my lips.
translator_notes: The phrase niskeihem mi-dam ('their drink offerings of blood') likely refers to pagan rituals involving blood libations, which were forbidden in Israelite worship. Some scholars see this as a reference to human sacrifice or to rituals involving the mingling of blood with wine in the drink offering.
The ambiguity of atsvotam ('their sorrows' or 'their idols') is productive: pursuing false gods generates both — the idols themselves and the misery they bring.

verses.text_hebrew: יְֽהוָ֗ה מְנָת־חֶלְקִ֥י וְכוֹסִ֑י אַ֝תָּ֗ה תּוֹמִ֥יךְ גּוֹרָלִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD is the portion of mine inheritance and of my cup: thou maintainest my lot.
verses.rendering:
The LORD is my allotted portion and my cup;
you hold my lot secure.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the theological center of the psalm. Four words of inheritance converge on God: menat chelqi ('the portion of my allotment'), kosi ('my cup'), and gorali ('my lot'). Every term comes from the vocabulary of land distribution. When Israel entered Canaan, each tribe received its nachalah by lot (goral). The chelqi ('portion, share') was the specific parcel assigned. The kos ('cup') metaphorically represents one's assigned destiny or experience. But the Levites received no land — 'The LORD is your portion and your inheritance' (Numbers 18:20). The psalmist here takes the Levitical exception and makes it a personal confession: where others have land, cattle, and property as their security, I have God. The final clause — attah tomikh gorali ('you hold my lot') — means God not only is the inheritance but secures it. The verb tamakh ('to hold, grasp, maintain') pictures God's hand firmly grasping the psalmist's portion, preventing it from being lost or stolen.
translator_notes: The four inheritance terms (menat, cheleq, kos, goral) are drawn from the concrete world of land allotment and applied to the relationship with God. This is not abstract theology but economic metaphor: the psalmist's wealth, security, and future are invested entirely in God.

key_terms.semantic_range: portion, share, allotment, inheritance, tract of land
key_terms.note: cheleq is the share one receives when property is divided. In Numbers 18:20 and Deuteronomy 10:9, the LORD is the Levites' cheleq because they receive no tribal land. The psalmist's claim that the LORD is his cheleq adopts Levitical theology as personal devotion: God replaces land as the source of security and identity.

key_terms.semantic_range: cup, goblet; metaphorically: one's assigned lot, destiny, experience
key_terms.note: kos as metaphor refers to what one is given to drink — one's destiny or experience. It can be a cup of blessing (Psalm 23:5) or a cup of wrath (Psalm 75:8). Here it is a cup of contentment: what God has poured for the psalmist is satisfying.

verses.text_hebrew: חֲבָלִ֗ים נָפְלוּ־לִ֥י בַּנְּעִמִ֑ים אַף־נַ֝חֲלָ֗ת שָׁפְרָ֥ה עָלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: The lines are fallen unto me in pleasant places; yea, I have a goodly heritage.
verses.rendering:
The boundary lines have fallen for me in pleasant places;
yes, my inheritance is beautiful to me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The land-allotment metaphor reaches its emotional peak. chavalim naflu li ba-ne'imim ('the measuring lines have fallen for me in pleasant places') — chavalim are the ropes used to measure property boundaries when dividing land. When the lot was cast and the lines were drawn, the psalmist's portion turned out to be in ne'imim — 'pleasant, delightful, lovely' places. The metaphor communicates deep satisfaction: whatever God has assigned me, it is beautiful. The second line intensifies: af nachalat shafrah alay ('yes, my inheritance is lovely upon me'). The verb shafar ('to be fair, beautiful, pleasing') describes aesthetic delight. The psalmist does not merely accept the inheritance — they find it beautiful. This is contentment without resignation: not 'I will make do with what I have' but 'what I have is genuinely lovely.' Since the inheritance is God himself (v. 5), the psalm is saying: God is beautiful to me.
translator_notes: chavalim ('lines, ropes, measuring cords') comes from the practice of dividing land with ropes. The idiom 'the lines have fallen' means 'my portion has been assigned.' The image is of a person standing on their newly measured property, looking around, and finding it genuinely delightful.

key_terms.semantic_range: inheritance, possession, hereditary property, allotted portion
key_terms.note: nachalah is the primary word for inherited property in the Hebrew Bible, especially the tribal allotments in Canaan. For the psalmist, nachalah is not land but God — an inheritance that cannot be taken, divided, or depleted.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲבָרֵ֗ךְ אֶת־יְ֭הוָה אֲשֶׁ֣ר יְעָצָ֑נִי אַף־לֵ֝יל֗וֹת יִסְּר֥וּנִי כִלְיוֹתָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I will bless the LORD, who hath given me counsel: my reins also instruct me in the night seasons.
verses.rendering:
I bless the LORD who counsels me;
even in the night my inner being instructs me.
translator_notes: kilayot ('kidneys') as the seat of deep thought and emotion appears in Jeremiah 17:10 ('I the LORD search the heart and examine the kidneys') and Psalm 139:13 ('you formed my kidneys'). Translating as 'inner being' preserves the function while avoiding the anatomical literalism.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁוִּ֬יתִי יְהוָ֣ה לְנֶגְדִּ֣י תָמִ֑יד כִּ֥י מִֽ֝ימִינִ֗י בַּל־אֶמּֽוֹט׃
verses.text_kjv: I have set the LORD always before me: because he is at my right hand, I shall not be moved.
verses.rendering:
I have set the LORD before me always;
because he is at my right hand, I will not be shaken.
translator_notes: The phrase shivviti YHVH le-negdi tamid became so central to Jewish spirituality that it was inscribed on plaques (called shivviti plaques) hung in synagogues and homes as a visual reminder to keep God constantly in view.

verses.text_hebrew: לָכֵ֤ן ׀ שָׂמַ֣ח לִ֭בִּי וַיָּ֣גֶל כְּבוֹדִ֑י אַף־בְּ֝שָׂרִ֗י יִשְׁכֹּ֥ן לָבֶֽטַח׃
verses.text_kjv: Therefore my heart is glad, and my glory rejoiceth: my flesh also shall rest in hope.
verses.rendering:
Therefore my heart is glad and my glory rejoices;
even my body rests secure.
verses.expanded_rendering: The lakhen ('therefore') draws the logical conclusion from everything preceding. Because God is my portion, my inheritance, my counselor, and my constant companion — therefore: samach libbi ('my heart is glad'), vayyagel kevodi ('and my glory rejoices'), af besari yishkon la-vetach ('even my flesh dwells securely'). The word kevod ('glory, honor, weight') here likely refers to the psalmist's soul or essential self — the 'glorious' part of a human being, the seat of dignity. The final clause reaches the body: even my flesh (basar) will dwell (yishkon) securely (la-vetach). The verb shakan ('to dwell, settle, tabernacle') is the same root from which mishkan ('tabernacle') derives. The body itself becomes a place of secure dwelling because God is near. The joy is comprehensive: heart, soul, and body are all at rest.
translator_notes: kevod ('glory') as a term for the human soul or essential self appears also in Psalm 7:5 and 57:8. The Septuagint translates it as 'my tongue' (glossa), perhaps reading kavod as kaved ('liver'). Peter's quotation in Acts 2:26 follows the Septuagint: 'my tongue rejoiced.'

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, honor, weight, dignity, splendor; when applied to a person, the essential dignified self
key_terms.note: kavod applied to a human being refers to that which gives the person weight and dignity — the soul or inner self at its most exalted. Here it is the part of the psalmist that rejoices alongside the heart, forming a pair that encompasses the full inner life.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י ׀ לֹא־תַעֲזֹ֣ב נַפְשִׁ֣י לִשְׁא֑וֹל לֹֽא־תִתֵּ֥ן חֲ֝סִידְךָ֗ לִרְא֥וֹת שָֽׁחַת׃
verses.text_kjv: For thou wilt not leave my soul in hell; neither wilt thou suffer thine Holy One to see corruption.
verses.rendering:
For you will not abandon my soul to Sheol,
nor will you let your faithful one see the pit.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the verse that reverberates through the entire biblical tradition. ki lo ta'azov nafshi li-She'ol ('for you will not abandon my soul to Sheol') — the verb azav ('to abandon, forsake, leave behind') is the verb of covenant betrayal. To abandon someone to Sheol is to let the relationship end at death — to allow the bond between God and the psalmist to be dissolved by the grave. The psalmist declares that God will not do this. The parallel line intensifies: lo titten chasidekha lir'ot shachat ('you will not give your faithful one to see the pit'). The chasid ('faithful one, devoted one') — the same word from Psalm 12:2 — is the person defined by chesed, by covenant loyalty. The shachat is the pit, the grave, the place of corruption and decay. The psalm's logic is relational: the bond between God and the chasid is too strong for death to sever. Whatever Sheol is, it cannot hold the one whom God loves. Peter will argue in Acts 2:27-31 that David could not have been speaking of himself (since David died and was buried), but was prophesying about someone whose body would not see decay.
translator_notes: She'ol in the Hebrew Bible is not hell in the later Christian sense. It is the shadowy underworld where all the dead go — not a place of punishment for the wicked but the default destination of all who die. The psalm's remarkable claim is not that the psalmist avoids punishment but that the psalmist avoids the normal fate of all humanity: descent into She'ol.

shachat can mean 'pit' (a place) or 'corruption, decay' (a process). The Septuagint translates it as diaphthora ('corruption, decay'), which becomes theologically significant in Peter's argument in Acts 2:31 — David's body did see decay, but the one David prophesied about did not.

key_terms.semantic_range: the underworld, the grave, the realm of the dead, the place beneath
key_terms.note: She'ol is the Hebrew Bible's term for the abode of the dead. It is not equivalent to the later concept of hell. All the dead were thought to descend there — righteous and wicked alike. The psalm's claim that God will not abandon the psalmist to She'ol pushes beyond standard Old Testament theology toward a hope that the relationship with God transcends death.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal one, devoted one, the one characterized by chesed
key_terms.note: The psalmist calls himself God's chasid — the one who practices and receives chesed. The claim is that the bond of faithful love between God and the chasid is permanent, surviving even death. This is covenant theology pushed to its ultimate conclusion.

verses.text_hebrew: תּֽוֹדִיעֵ֗נִי אֹ֣רַח חַ֭יִּים שֹׂ֣בַע שְׂמָח֣וֹת אֶת־פָּנֶ֑יךָ נְעִמ֖וֹת בִּימִינְךָ֣ נֶֽצַח׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou wilt shew me the path of life: in thy presence is fulness of joy; at thy right hand there are pleasures for evermore.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse is among the most luminous in all of Scripture. todi'eni orach chayyim ('you make known to me the path of life') — the verb yada (Hiphil, 'to cause to know, to reveal') means God actively discloses the way that leads to life. orach chayyim ('the path of life') is both a wisdom concept (the way of living well) and an eschatological hope (the way that leads beyond death to life with God). sova semachot et panekha ('fullness of joy in your presence') — sova means 'satiation, fullness to the point of satisfaction.' The joy is not partial but complete, and its source is God's face (panekha) — the direct, unmediated presence of God that Psalm 11:7 promised the upright would behold. ne'imot biminekha netsach ('pleasures at your right hand forever') — the ne'imot ('pleasures, delights') are located at God's right hand, the position of honor and intimacy. And the word netsach ('forever, perpetually, to the end') means these pleasures have no expiration. The psalm that began with a plea for protection ends with a vision of eternal delight in God's presence.
translator_notes: The progression from 'path of life' to 'fullness of joy' to 'pleasures forever' creates an escalating trajectory that carries the reader beyond the boundaries of this life into something that sounds like eternal communion with God. Whether the original psalmist understood this as life after death in the later theological sense is debated, but the language strains toward a reality that death cannot contain.

This verse has been called the high-water mark of Old Testament hope. It does not describe survival after death in explicit terms, but it describes a relationship with God so full, so joyful, and so permanent that death seems unable to interrupt it.

key_terms.semantic_range: way, road, path; life, living, vitality — together: the course that leads to true and lasting life
key_terms.note: orach chayyim appears in Proverbs as a wisdom concept (the way of wise living) and here takes on deeper resonance — a path that leads through death into God's presence. The phrase becomes a portal between wisdom theology and resurrection hope.
